title: Rename single-letter variables in BTreeViz for teaching readability
description: btree-viz.ts uses 'i' as key comparison index (lines 105-108, 140-161), 'cl' as child layout in renderer (DatabaseModule.tsx:782). Rename: i→keyIndex in searchNode and insertNonFull, newNode→rightSibling in splitChild, cl→childLayout in BTreeCanvas renderer. Code should be readable WITHOUT comments. Source: Sedgewick algs4 BTree.java uses 'numberOfEntries', 'children' — descriptive names.
acceptanceCriteria: Zero single-letter variable names in btree-viz.ts (except standard loop counters in trivial loops)
Key comparison index named 'keyIndex' in searchNode and insertNonFull
Split result named 'rightSibling' instead of 'newNode'
All variables in BTreeCanvas renderer use full descriptive names

title: Add inline WHY comments to BTreeViz splice operations
description: btree-viz.ts:195-211 splitChild uses 3 consecutive splice() calls that are correct but opaque. A student reading this code cannot understand WHY each splice happens. Add inline comments explaining: (1) splice(midIndex+1) extracts RIGHT half of keys for new sibling, (2) splice(midIndex) removes the MEDIAN key which will be promoted, (3) splice(midIndex+1) on children moves RIGHT half of children to new sibling. Also add 'why' comment to line 143: 'Insert into leaf in sorted order — find position by scanning from right'.
acceptanceCriteria: Every splice() call in splitChild has a comment explaining WHAT it does and WHY
Comments form a narrative: 'Step 1: Extract right half → Step 2: Remove median → Step 3: Move children'
The insertNonFull leaf insertion (line 143-147) has a comment explaining the right-to-left scan
A student reading ONLY the comments can understand the split algorithm

title: Handle duplicate key insertion in BTreeViz
description: btree-viz.ts insertNonFull (line 135-181) inserts duplicate keys without checking if the key already exists. CLRS B-Trees assume unique keys. Inserting duplicates produces a tree with multiple copies of the same key, which can confuse search (returns first found). Add: (1) Check if key exists during insert, (2) If exists, generate a step: 'Key X already exists — B-Trees require unique keys. Skipping.', (3) Return without inserting.
acceptanceCriteria: Inserting a duplicate key produces a 'Key already exists' step instead of inserting
The tree is not modified on duplicate insert
Search for a key after attempted duplicate insert returns correctly
Step description explains WHY duplicates are rejected

title: Add B-Tree deletion operation to BTreeViz
description: BTreeViz only has insert and search. Deletion is missing entirely. CLRS Ch18 covers B-Tree deletion with 3 cases: (1) key in leaf — remove directly, (2) key in internal node — replace with predecessor/successor and delete from child, (3) child has minimum keys — merge or redistribute before descending. Add delete() method with step-by-step trace.
acceptanceCriteria: BTreeViz.delete(key) method returns BTreeStep[] trace
Handles leaf deletion (direct remove)
Handles internal node deletion (predecessor replacement)
Handles underflow: merge or redistribute when child has minimum keys
Delete button and input field added to B-Tree sidebar controls
Step descriptions explain each case: 'Key found in leaf — removing directly'

title: Add open addressing variant (linear probing) to HashIndexViz
description: HashIndexViz only implements separate chaining. Open addressing (linear probing) is the other major collision resolution strategy and is taught in every algorithms course. Add: (1) LinearProbingHashViz class, (2) On collision, probe next bucket sequentially, (3) Clustering demonstration: show how linear probing creates clusters, (4) Toggle in UI between 'Separate Chaining' and 'Linear Probing'. Source: CLRS Ch11, Sedgewick Ch3.4.
acceptanceCriteria: LinearProbingHashViz class with insert/search/delete returning step traces
Visualization shows probing sequence: 'Bucket 3 occupied → trying bucket 4 → trying bucket 5 → inserted'
Clustering visible: consecutive occupied buckets highlighted as a cluster
Toggle in sidebar between Separate Chaining and Linear Probing
Comparison metric: average probe length for each strategy

title: Add delete/tombstone support to LSMTreeViz
description: LSMTreeViz has no delete operation. In real LSM-Trees, deletes write a TOMBSTONE marker to the memtable, which suppresses the key during reads and is cleaned up during compaction. This is a key concept for understanding LSM-Trees. Add: (1) delete(key) writes a tombstone entry, (2) Read returns 'deleted' when tombstone is found, (3) Compaction removes tombstoned entries. Source: DDIA Ch3, LevelDB documentation.
acceptanceCriteria: LSMTreeViz.delete(key) method writes a tombstone marker to memtable
Read returns 'Key deleted (tombstone found)' step when tombstone encountered
Compaction removes entries with matching tombstones
Delete button added to LSM-Tree sidebar controls
Step description explains: 'Deletes in LSM-Trees don't remove data — they write a tombstone that hides the old value'

title: Add leveled compaction strategy variant to LSMTreeViz
description: LSMTreeViz only implements size-tiered compaction (merge all SSTables in a level). Real databases (LevelDB, RocksDB) primarily use LEVELED compaction where only overlapping SSTables are merged. Add: (1) LeveledCompaction variant where each level has non-overlapping key ranges, (2) Compaction selects ONE L0 SSTable and merges with overlapping L1 SSTables, (3) Toggle between 'Size-Tiered' and 'Leveled' strategies. Source: RocksDB compaction docs, Database Internals Ch7.
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle in sidebar between Size-Tiered and Leveled compaction
Leveled compaction: each level has non-overlapping key ranges (visible in SSTable labels)
Compaction selects specific overlapping SSTables (highlighted), not the entire level
Step description explains: 'Leveled compaction reduces read amplification by maintaining sorted runs per level'
Comparison metric: read vs write amplification for each strategy

title: Add legends to BTree, Hash, LSM, and Transaction canvases
description: 4 out of 5 main canvases have NO legend explaining what colors/badges mean. Only QueryPlanCanvas has a 3-state legend (Low/Med/High). Add: BTree legend (insert=green, split=amber, search=blue), Hash legend (hash=violet, insert=green, collision=amber, resize=rose, delete=red, search=blue), LSM legend (write=green, flush=amber, compact=violet, read=blue), Transaction legend (normal=neutral, anomaly=red pulse).
acceptanceCriteria: BTreeCanvas shows legend bar with 3 operation colors + descriptions
HashIndexCanvas shows legend bar with 6 operation colors + descriptions
LSMCanvas shows legend bar with 4 operation colors + descriptions
TransactionCanvas shows legend for normal step vs anomaly step
Legends are compact (single row) and don't obscure the canvas

title: Add pseudocode display for B-Tree, Hash Index, and LSM-Tree algorithms
description: Zero pseudocode exists for any algorithm. Students can't map visualization steps to formal algorithm logic. Add collapsible pseudocode panel in Properties view for: (1) B-Tree INSERT (CLRS Ch18 style), (2) B-Tree SEARCH, (3) Hash Insert with chaining, (4) LSM Write→Flush→Compact pipeline. Highlight the CURRENT pseudocode line during step-through animation. Source: CLRS Ch18 pseudocode, Sedgewick Ch3.4.
acceptanceCriteria: Pseudocode panel visible in Properties for B-Tree, Hash Index, LSM-Tree modes
B-Tree has INSERT and SEARCH pseudocode matching CLRS notation
Hash Index has INSERT pseudocode with collision handling
LSM-Tree has WRITE/FLUSH/COMPACT pseudocode
Current step highlights the corresponding pseudocode line (if in step-through mode)
Pseudocode uses standard notation (not TypeScript syntax)

title: Add Python display code for B-Tree and Hash Index implementations
description: All implementations are TypeScript-only. Students learning in Python or Java can't read the code. Add Python display code that matches the TypeScript implementation logic: (1) B-Tree insert/search in Python with descriptive variable names, (2) Hash table with separate chaining in Python. Display as syntax-highlighted code panel alongside visualization. Source: GeeksforGeeks B-Tree Python, Real Python Hash Table tutorial.
acceptanceCriteria: Python tab visible in Properties panel for B-Tree and Hash Index modes
Python code matches the TypeScript implementation logic step-by-step
Variable names are Pythonic (snake_case): 'key_index', 'right_sibling', 'bucket_index'
Syntax highlighting with line numbers
Current line highlighted during step-through (matching pseudocode behavior)

title: Add write skew and lost update scenarios to transaction simulation
description: transaction-sim.ts only has 4 scenarios (1 per isolation level × 1 anomaly each). Missing: (1) Write Skew — two transactions read overlapping data and make conflicting writes (doctor on-call scenario from DDIA Ch7), only prevented by SERIALIZABLE. (2) Lost Update — two transactions read-modify-write the same row, one update is silently lost. Add as additional scenarios selectable from sidebar. Source: DDIA Ch7, PostgreSQL MVCC docs.
acceptanceCriteria: Write Skew scenario added: two doctors both check on-call count, both go off-call, violating minimum constraint
Lost Update scenario added: two transactions increment a counter, one increment is lost
Both scenarios accessible via new buttons in Transaction Isolation sidebar
Step descriptions explain WHY each anomaly occurs and which isolation level prevents it
Write Skew description notes: 'Only SERIALIZABLE prevents this — Repeatable Read is NOT sufficient'

title: Add real-world production comparison panels for B-Tree, Hash, and LSM
description: Zero connection between our teaching implementations and real database code. Add 'In Production' collapsible panel for: (1) B-Tree: 'PostgreSQL nbtree uses B+ Trees with page-level locking. Our version is simplified: no pages, no locking, no concurrent access.', (2) Hash: 'PostgreSQL hash indexes use linear hashing. Python dict uses open addressing with random probing.', (3) LSM: 'RocksDB adds: WAL, bloom filters, block cache, compression, rate limiting. Our version shows the core pipeline.' Source: PostgreSQL source docs, RocksDB wiki.
acceptanceCriteria: Production comparison panel in Properties for B-Tree, Hash Index, LSM-Tree
Each panel: 'Our version vs Production version' with 3+ differences listed
Differences framed as teaching moments: 'We simplified X because the concept is Y'
At least one link to real source code or documentation per topic
